{{Short description|Changes in microbiota leading to disease}}

 |synonyms        = dysbacteriosis

 |alt             =

 |caption         = 
 |width           =

 |image_size2     = 
 |alt2            = 
 |image_thumbtime2 =
 |caption2        = 
 |width2          = 
 |pronounce       = 
 |pronounce 2     = 
 |specialty       = <!-- from Wikidata; can be overwritten -->
 |symptoms        = 
 |complications   = 
 |onset           = 
 |duration        = 
 |types           = 
 |causes          = <!-- or |cause= -->
 |risks           = <!-- or |risk= -->
 |diagnosis       = 
 |differential    = 
 |prevention      = 
 |treatment       = [[Fecal microbiota transplant|Fecal Microbiota Transplantation]], [[antibiotics]], [[Probiotics]]
 |medication      = 
 |prognosis       = 
 |frequency       = <!-- also |incidence= or |prevalence= -->
 |deaths          = 
}}
'''Dysbiosis''' (also called '''dysbacteriosis''') is characterized by a disruption to the [[microbiome]] resulting in an imbalance in the [[microbiota]], changes in their functional composition and metabolic activities, or a shift in their local distribution.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Tamboli CP, Neut C, Desreumaux P, Colombel JF | title = Dysbiosis in inflammatory bowel disease | journal = Gut | volume = 53 | issue = 1 | pages = 1–4 | date = January 2004 | pmid = 14684564 | pmc = 1773911 | doi = 10.1136/gut.53.1.1 }}</ref><ref name="Moos_2016">{{cite journal | vauthors = Moos WH, Faller DV, Harpp DN, Kanara I, Pernokas J, Powers WR, Steliou K | title = Microbiota and Neurological Disorders: A Gut Feeling | journal = BioResearch Open Access | volume = 5 | issue = 1 | pages = 137–45 | year = 2016 | pmid = 27274912 | pmc = 4892191 | doi = 10.1089/biores.2016.0010 | quote = As reviewed in this report, synthetic biology shows potential in developing microorganisms for correcting pathogenic dysbiosis (gut microbiota-host maladaptation), although this has yet to be proven. }}</ref> For example, a part of the [[human microbiota]] such as the [[skin flora]], [[gut flora]], or [[vaginal flora]], can become deranged (unbalanced), when normally dominating [[species]] become underrepresented and species that normally are outcompeted or contained increase to fill the void. Due to perceived implications in diverse diseases, there has been a rapid growth in research and innovation interest in different approaches to counteract dysbiosis.<ref>El Boukhari R, Matin M, Bouissane L, Ławiński M, Lushchak O, Singla RK, Mickael ME, Mayneris-Perxachs J, Grafakou ME, Xu S, Liu B, Guan J, Półtorak A, Szpicer A, Wierzbicka A, Tzvetkov NT, Banach M, Horbańczuk JO, Jóźwik A, Cascella M, Shen B, Pirgozliev VR, Wang D, Litvinova O, Adamska O, Kamińska A, Łapiński M, Stolarczyk A, Berindan-Neagoe I, Milella L, Yeung AWK, Suravajhala P, Bishayee A, Lordan R, Iantovics LB, Lagoa R, Michalczuk M, Stoyanov J, Kinghorn AD, Jalil B, Weckwerth W, Goh BH, Li MY, Chaubey G, Russo GL, Frazzini S, Rossi L, Battino M, Jia W, Su Q, Ma X, Rollinger JM, Rittmann SKR, Sheridan H, Walsh JJ, Lizard G, Karpiński TM, Silva AS, Piwowarski J, Xie L, Fan TP, Giampieri F, El Midaoui A, Wong KH, Gan RY, Fatimi A, Atanasov AG. [https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12130569/ Enhancing human gut health: Global innovations in dysbiosis management]. Imeta. 2025 Apr 13;4(3):e70028. [https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/imt2.70028 doi: 10.1002/imt2.70028]. PMID: 40469512; PMCID: PMC12130569.</ref> Similar to the human gut microbiome, diverse microbes colonize the plant [[rhizosphere]], and dysbiosis in the rhizosphere, can negatively impact plant health.<ref name=tk>{{cite journal |last1=Ketehouli |first1=T |last2=Pasche |first2=J |last3=Buttros |first3=V |last4=Goss |first4=E |last5=Martins |first5=SJ |title=The underground world of plant disease: Rhizosphere dysbiosis reduces above-ground plant resistance to bacterial leaf spot and alters plant transcriptome |journal=Environmental Microbiology |date=2025 |volume=26 |issue=7 |article-number=e16676 |doi=10.1111/1462-2920.16676 |pmid=39010309 |doi-access=free }}</ref> Dysbiosis is most commonly reported as a condition in the [[gastrointestinal tract]]<ref name="Moos_2016" /> or [[rhizosphere|plant rhizosphere]].<ref name=tk/>

Typical microbial colonies found on or in the body are benign or [[probiotic|beneficial]]. These appropriately sized microbial colonies carry out a series of helpful and necessary functions, such as aiding in [[digestion]].<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Kau AL, Ahern PP, Griffin NW, Goodman AL, Gordon JI | title = Human nutrition, the gut microbiome and the immune system | journal = Nature | volume = 474 | issue = 7351 | pages = 327–36 | date = June 2011 | pmid = 21677749 | pmc = 3298082 | doi = 10.1038/nature10213 }}</ref>  They also help protect the body from infiltration by [[pathogenic]] microbes. These beneficial microbial colonies compete with each other for space and resources.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Xuan C, Shamonki JM, Chung A, Dinome ML, Chung M, Sieling PA, Lee DJ | title = Microbial dysbiosis is associated with human breast cancer | journal = PLOS ONE | volume = 9 | issue = 1 | article-number = e83744 | date = 2014-01-08 | pmid = 24421902 | pmc = 3885448 | doi = 10.1371/journal.pone.0083744 | bibcode = 2014PLoSO...983744X | doi-access = free }}</ref> However, when this balance is disturbed, these colonies exhibit a decreased ability to check each other's growth, which can then lead to overgrowth of one or more of the disturbed colonies which may further damage some of the other smaller beneficial ones in a [[vicious cycle]]. As more beneficial colonies are damaged, making the imbalance more pronounced, more overgrowth issues occur because the damaged colonies are less able to check the growth of the overgrowing ones.  If this goes unchecked long enough, a pervasive and [[chronic (medicine)|chronic]] imbalance between colonies will set in, which ultimately minimizes the beneficial nature of these colonies as a whole.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = DeGruttola AK, Low D, Mizoguchi A, Mizoguchi E | title = Current Understanding of Dysbiosis in Disease in Human and Animal Models | journal = Inflammatory Bowel Diseases | volume = 22 | issue = 5 | pages = 1137–1150 | date = May 2016 | pmid = 27070911 | pmc = 4838534 | doi = 10.1097/MIB.0000000000000750 }}</ref>

== Potential causes of dysbiosis ==
Any disruption of the body's microbiota is able to lead to dysbiosis. Dysbiosis in the gut happens when the bacteria in the gastrointestinal tract become unbalanced.<ref>{{Cite web |title=What Is Dysbiosis? |url=https://www.webmd.com/digestive-disorders/what-is-dysbiosis |access-date=2022-12-10 |website=WebMD |language=en |archive-date=2022-02-02 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220202065832/https://www.webmd.com/digestive-disorders/what-is-dysbiosis |url-status=live }}</ref> There are many causes for dysbiosis in the gut. Some reasons include, but are not limited to:  

* Dietary changes<ref name="Dysbiosis in intestinal inflammatio">{{cite journal | vauthors = Buttó LF, Haller D | title = Dysbiosis in intestinal inflammation: Cause or consequence | journal = International Journal of Medical Microbiology | volume = 306 | issue = 5 | pages = 302–309 | date = August 2016 | pmid = 27012594 | doi = 10.1016/j.ijmm.2016.02.010 }}</ref>
* [[Antibiotic]]s that affect the [[gut flora]]<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Burgos |first1=F. |last2=Cai |first2=W. |last3=Arias |first3=C. |title= Gut dysbiosis induced by florfenicol increases susceptibility to Aeromonas hydrophila infection in zebrafish after the recommended withdrawal period |journal=Journal of Aquatic Animal Health |year=2024 |volume=36 |issue=2 |doi=10.1002/aah.10211|doi-access=free |hdl=2031/9be7e0be-0ab4-4183-90c5-568ff363213a |hdl-access=free }}</ref><ref name="pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov">{{cite journal | vauthors = Hawrelak JA, Myers SP | title = The causes of intestinal dysbiosis: a review | journal = Alternative Medicine Review | volume = 9 | issue = 2 | pages = 180–197 | date = June 2004 | pmid = 15253677 }}</ref><ref name="Dysbiosis in intestinal inflammatio"/>
* Psychological and physical [[Stress (biology)|stress]] (weakens [[immune system]])<ref name="pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov"/>
* Use of [[radiation]], [[chemotherapy]], [[antiviral drug]]s, radioactive isotopes, and [[hormone therapy]]<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Wang Z, Wang Q, Wang X, Zhu L, Chen J, Zhang B, Chen Y, Yuan Z | display-authors = 6 | title = Gut microbial dysbiosis is associated with development and progression of radiation enteritis during pelvic radiotherapy | journal = Journal of Cellular and Molecular Medicine | volume = 23 | issue = 5 | pages = 3747–3756 | date = May 2019 | pmid = 30908851 | pmc = 6484301 | doi = 10.1111/jcmm.14289 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Luo N, Zhu W, Li X, Fu M, Peng X, Yang F, Zhang Y, Yin H, Yang C, Zhao J, Yuan X, Hu G | display-authors = 6 | title = Impact of Gut Microbiota on Radiation-Associated Cognitive Dysfunction and Neuroinflammation in Mice | journal = Radiation Research | volume = 197 | issue = 4 | pages = 350–364 | date = April 2022 | pmid = 34982167 | doi = 10.1667/RADE-21-00006.1 | s2cid = 245670201 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Liu J, Liu C, Yue J | title = Radiotherapy and the gut microbiome: facts and fiction | journal = Radiation Oncology | volume = 16 | issue = 1 | article-number = 9 | date = January 2021 | pmid = 33436010 | pmc = 7805150 | doi = 10.1186/s13014-020-01735-9 | doi-access = free }}</ref>
* Presence of intestinal [[helminths]] ([[human parasite]]s)<ref>{{Cite web |vauthors=Yaneff J |date=2018-05-22 |title=How to Deal with Dysbiosis Microbial Imbalance in the Body |url=https://www.doctorshealthpress.com/dysbiosis-microbial-imbalance/ |access-date=2022-12-10 |website=Doctors Health Press – Daily Free Health Articles and Natural Health Advice |language=en-US |archive-date=2022-12-10 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221210155127/https://www.doctorshealthpress.com/dysbiosis-microbial-imbalance/ |url-status=live }}</ref>
* Uncontrolled rectal cleansing with [[enema]]s<ref>{{Cite web |title=Enemas |url=https://www.doctorsbeyondmedicine.com/listing/enemas |access-date=2022-12-10 |website=www.doctorsbeyondmedicine.com |archive-date=2022-12-10 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221210155129/https://www.doctorsbeyondmedicine.com/listing/enemas |url-status=live }}</ref>
* [[Inflammatory bowel disease|Inflammatory]] process in the intestine <ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Zeng MY, Inohara N, Nuñez G | title = Mechanisms of inflammation-driven bacterial dysbiosis in the gut | journal = Mucosal Immunology | volume = 10 | issue = 1 | pages = 18–26 | date = January 2017 | pmid = 27554295 | pmc = 5788567 | doi = 10.1038/mi.2016.75 }}</ref>
* Chronic and acute infections (HIV, [[Hepatitis C]] & B, herpes, etc.)<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Pham TA, Lawley TD | title = Emerging insights on intestinal dysbiosis during bacterial infections | journal = Current Opinion in Microbiology | volume = 17 | issue = 100 | pages = 67–74 | date = February 2014 | pmid = 24581695 | pmc = 3969284 | doi = 10.1016/j.mib.2013.12.002 }}</ref><ref name="Dysbiosis in intestinal inflammatio"/>

=== Gut/intestinal dysbiosis ===
Bacteria in the human gut's intestines are the most diverse in the human body and play a vital role in human health. In the [[gastrointestinal tract]], dysbiosis manifests particularly during [[small intestinal bacterial overgrowth]] (SIBO), commonly caused by a decrease in the passage of food and waste through the gastrointestinal tract following surgery or other pre-existing conditions.<ref name="SIBO">{{Cite web |title=Small intestinal bacterial overgrowth (SIBO) – Symptoms and causes |url=https://www.mayoclinic.org/diseases-conditions/small-intestinal-bacterial-overgrowth/symptoms-causes/syc-20370168 |access-date=2022-11-28 |website=Mayo Clinic |language=en |archive-date=2022-11-28 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221128151358/https://www.mayoclinic.org/diseases-conditions/small-intestinal-bacterial-overgrowth/symptoms-causes/syc-20370168 |url-status=live }}</ref> SIBO is characterized by symptoms of [[abdominal pain]], [[diarrhea]], discomfort after eating, and malnutrition.<ref name="SIBO" /> Similarly, dysbiosis manifests during small intestinal fungal overgrowth (SIFO) caused by excessive population levels of fungi in a bowel.<ref name="Singh_2017">{{cite journal | vauthors = Singh R, Mullin GE | title = A Wasting Syndrome and Malnutrition Caused by Small Intestine Fungal Overgrowth: Case Report and Review of the Literature | journal = Integrative Medicine | volume = 16 | issue = 3 | pages = 48–51 | date = June 2017 | pmid = 30881247 | pmc = 6419785 }}</ref> SIFO can be characterized by GI symptoms (vomiting, diarrhea) in those previously immunocompromised.<ref name="Singh_2017" /><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Erdogan A, Rao SS | title = Small intestinal fungal overgrowth | journal = Current Gastroenterology Reports | volume = 17 | issue = 4 | article-number = 16 | date = April 2015 | pmid = 25786900 | doi = 10.1007/s11894-015-0436-2 | s2cid = 3098136 }}</ref> The consumer's dietary habits can be one of the most influential factors on the gut's microbiota.<ref name="Carding_2015">{{cite journal | vauthors = Carding S, Verbeke K, Vipond DT, Corfe BM, Owen LJ | title = Dysbiosis of the gut microbiota in disease | journal = Microbial Ecology in Health and Disease | volume = 26 | article-number = 26191 | date = February 2015 | pmid = 25651997 | pmc = 4315779 | doi = 10.3402/mehd.v26.26191 }}</ref> Diets high in carbohydrates and refined sugars are common links to dysbiosis in the gut, whereas those rich in fruits, vegetables, and fish oils are considered more favorable to the gut due to their anti-inflammatory properties.<ref name="Tomasello_2017">{{cite journal | vauthors = Tomasello G, Mazzola M, Leone A, Sinagra E, Zummo G, Farina F, Damiani P, Cappello F, Gerges Geagea A, Jurjus A, Bou Assi T, Messina M, Carini F | display-authors = 6 | title = Nutrition, oxidative stress and intestinal dysbiosis: Influence of diet on gut microbiota in inflammatory bowel diseases | journal = Biomedical Papers of the Medical Faculty of the University Palacky, Olomouc, Czechoslovakia | volume = 160 | issue = 4 | pages = 461–466 | date = December 2016 | pmid = 27812084 | doi = 10.5507/bp.2016.052 | doi-access = free }}</ref> Many diseases, such as IBD, Type 2 Diabetes, Crohn's, and even allergies, are suggested to be due, in part, to an alteration in the microbiome of the gut.<ref name="Tomasello_2017" /><ref name="Carding_2015"/> Probiotics can sometimes cause mild gas and bloating in people who first start taking them, especially at high doses, as their body gets used to having new gut bacteria introduced into their gut.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Iqbal |date=2023-09-28 |title=Provitalize Probiotics? Are They Effective? |url=https://medictub.com/provitalize-probiotics/ |access-date=2023-09-29 |website=medictub |language=en-US}}</ref>

=== Oral dysbiosis ===
The mouth is frequently exposed to novel microbes from the environment, and this can lead to microbial disturbances in the mouth as well as in the stomach and intestines.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Koliarakis I, Messaritakis I, Nikolouzakis TK, Hamilos G, Souglakos J, Tsiaoussis J | title = Oral Bacteria and Intestinal Dysbiosis in Colorectal Cancer | journal = International Journal of Molecular Sciences | volume = 20 | issue = 17 | page = 4146 | date = August 2019 | pmid = 31450675 | pmc = 6747549 | doi = 10.3390/ijms20174146 | doi-access = free }}</ref> Hygiene and nutritional variation are imperative in preventing oral diseases such as [[gingivitis]], [[tooth decay]], and cavities, which are linked to altered microbial communities in the oral cavity.<ref name="Chimenos-Küstner_2017">{{cite journal | vauthors = Chimenos-Küstner E, Giovannoni ML, Schemel-Suárez M | title = Dysbiosis as a determinant factor of systemic and oral pathology: importance of microbiome | journal = Medicina Clinica | volume = 149 | issue = 7 | pages = 305–309 | date = October 2017 | pmid = 28669517 | doi = 10.1016/j.medcli.2017.05.036 | hdl-access = free | hdl = 2445/116548 }}</ref> Oral pathogens can affect multiple microbiota compartments of the body and alter systemic processes, such as immunological alterations or digestion issues. Smoking, drinking, oral intercourse, and advanced age are all associated with oral dysbiosis.<ref name="Chimenos-Küstner_2017" />

=== Skin dysbiosis ===
There are a number of types of microorganisms that reside in and on the human skin, collectively known as the [[skin flora]]. Normal healthy microbial communities may have some positive effects.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Grice EA, Segre JA | title = The skin microbiome | journal = Nature Reviews. Microbiology | volume = 9 | issue = 4 | pages = 244–253 | date = April 2011 | pmid = 21407241 | pmc = 3535073 | doi = 10.1038/nrmicro2537 }}</ref> Altered microbial composition and diversity (dysbiosis), may play a role in some non-infectious skin conditions such as [[acne]],<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Holland KT, Cunliffe WJ, Roberts CD | title = Acne vulgaris: an investigation into the number of anaerobic diphtheroids and members of the Micrococcaceae in normal and acne skin | journal = The British Journal of Dermatology | volume = 96 | issue = 6 | pages = 623–626 | date = June 1977 | pmid = 141301 | doi = 10.1111/j.1365-2133.1977.tb05206.x | s2cid = 37507292 }}</ref> [[atopic dermatitis]],<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Baker BS | title = The role of microorganisms in atopic dermatitis | journal = Clinical and Experimental Immunology | volume = 144 | issue = 1 | pages = 1–9 | date = April 2006 | pmid = 16542358 | pmc = 1809642 | doi = 10.1111/j.1365-2249.2005.02980.x }}</ref> [[psoriasis]],<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Paulino LC, Tseng CH, Strober BE, Blaser MJ | title = Molecular analysis of fungal microbiota in samples from healthy human skin and psoriatic lesions | journal = Journal of Clinical Microbiology | volume = 44 | issue = 8 | pages = 2933–2941 | date = August 2006 | pmid = 16891514 | pmc = 1594634 | doi = 10.1128/JCM.00785-06 }}</ref> and [[rosacea]].<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Grice EA | title = The skin microbiome: potential for novel diagnostic and therapeutic approaches to cutaneous disease | journal = Seminars in Cutaneous Medicine and Surgery | volume = 33 | issue = 2 | pages = 98–103 | date = June 2014 | pmid = 25085669 | pmc = 4425451 | doi = 10.12788/j.sder.0087 | doi-broken-date = 15 July 2025 }}</ref> In more extreme cases, such as [[cellulitis]], a pathogenic bacteria can infect the skin, the most common being [[Streptococcus|Streptococci]] species and [[Staphylococcus aureus]].<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Vary JC, O'Connor KM | title = Common dermatologic conditions | journal = The Medical Clinics of North America | volume = 98 | issue = 3 | pages = 445–485 | date = May 2014 | pmid = 24758956 | doi = 10.1016/j.mcna.2014.01.005 }}</ref>

=== Vaginal dysbiosis ===
The vagina contains a microbiome ([[vaginal flora]]) that can become disturbed and result in conditions such as [[bacterial vaginosis]]. Alterations in vaginal flora can also affect vaginal health in reproduction, as well as one's risk of acquiring and the subsequent severity of sexually transmitted infections.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Lewis FM, Bernstein KT, Aral SO | title = Vaginal Microbiome and Its Relationship to Behavior, Sexual Health, and Sexually Transmitted Diseases | journal = Obstetrics and Gynecology | volume = 129 | issue = 4 | pages = 643–654 | date = April 2017 | pmid = 28277350 | pmc = 6743080 | doi = 10.1097/AOG.0000000000001932 }}</ref>

=== Role of antibiotics in promoting dysbiosis ===
{{See also|Antibiotic misuse}}
Dysbiosis can occur during many stages of life and can be triggered by many different sources. Antibiotics, for example, are often a significant contributor to disruptions in microbiomes. This occurs because not all microbes will be affected by the antibiotic in the same way, and so it can change the balance of different types of microbes as well as changing the total number of microbes. Antibiotic usage during young childhood development can lead to adverse gut issues (dysbiosis) in adulthood.<ref name="Vangay_2015">{{cite journal | vauthors = Vangay P, Ward T, Gerber JS, Knights D | title = Antibiotics, pediatric dysbiosis, and disease | journal = Cell Host & Microbe | volume = 17 | issue = 5 | pages = 553–564 | date = May 2015 | pmid = 25974298 | pmc = 5555213 | doi = 10.1016/j.chom.2015.04.006 }}</ref> The [[gut microbiome]] is altered from antibiotics and is linked to future gut disease, i.e., [[Inflammatory bowel disease|IBD]], [[ulcerative colitis]], obesity, etc. The intestinal immune system is directly influenced by the gut microbiome and can be hard to recover if damaged through antibiotics.<ref name="Vangay_2015" /> The use of [[minocycline]] in acne vulgaris has been associated with skin and gut dysbiosis.<ref name="Thompson_2020">{{cite journal | vauthors = Thompson KG, Rainer BM, Antonescu C, Florea L, Mongodin EF, Kang S, Chien AL | title = Minocycline and Its Impact on Microbial Dysbiosis in the Skin and Gastrointestinal Tract of Acne Patients | journal = Annals of Dermatology | volume = 32 | issue = 1 | pages = 21–30 | date = February 2020 | pmid = 33911705 | pmc = 7992645 | doi = 10.5021/ad.2020.32.1.21 | doi-access = free }}</ref>

== Effects ==
Gut dysbiosis has been linked to the pathogenesis of both intestinal and extra-intestinal disorders.<ref name="Carding_2015"/> Dysbiosis may affect intestinal disorders include [[Inflammatory bowel disease|IBD]], [[Irritable bowel syndrome|IBS]], and [[coeliac disease]], as well as extra-intestinal conditions including allergies, asthma, [[metabolic syndrome]], cardiovascular disease, and obesity.<ref name="Carding_2015"/>

Gut dysbiosis can also be a factor in neurodegenerative and cerebrovascular diseases due to the link between age-related dysbiosis and inflammation. Inflammation is a common factor for a wide variety of age-related pathologies, including neurological diseases.<ref name="Rogers_2016">{{cite journal | vauthors = Rogers GB, Keating DJ, Young RL, Wong ML, Licinio J, Wesselingh S | title = From gut dysbiosis to altered brain function and mental illness: mechanisms and pathways | journal = Molecular Psychiatry | volume = 21 | issue = 6 | pages = 738–48 | date = June 2016 | pmid = 27090305 | pmc = 4879184 | doi = 10.1038/mp.2016.50 }}</ref> By correcting the dysbiosis in elderly patients, it may be possible to prevent the development of neurodegenerative diseases.<ref name="Koszewicz_2021">{{cite journal | vauthors = Koszewicz M, Jaroch J, Brzecka A, Ejma M, Budrewicz S, Mikhaleva LM, Muresanu C, Schield P, Somasundaram SG, Kirkland CE, Avila-Rodriguez M, Aliev G | display-authors = 6 | title = Dysbiosis is one of the risk factor for stroke and cognitive impairment and potential target for treatment | journal = Pharmacological Research | volume = 164 | article-number = 105277 | date = February 2021 | pmid = 33166735 | doi = 10.1016/j.phrs.2020.105277 | s2cid = 226296498 }}</ref> Dysbiosis may contribute to the cause or development of neurological conditions, including autism, pain, depression, anxiety, and stroke.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Kigerl KA, Hall JC, Wang L, Mo X, Yu Z, Popovich PG | title = Gut dysbiosis impairs recovery after spinal cord injury | journal = The Journal of Experimental Medicine | volume = 213 | issue = 12 | pages = 2603–2620 | date = November 2016 | pmid = 27810921 | pmc = 5110012 | doi = 10.1084/jem.20151345 }}</ref> Dysbiosis contributing to neurological conditions is due to interactions with the gut-brain axis allowing the gut microbiome to influence neural development, cognition, and behavior.<ref name="Rogers_2016"/> There has also been evidence that the gut microbiota composition can be altered due to changes in behavior, and changing the microbiome can also cause depressive-like behaviors.<ref name="Rogers_2016"/>

Microbial colonies also excrete many different types of waste byproducts.<ref name="Carding_2015"/> Using different waste removal mechanisms, under normal circumstances the body effectively manages these byproducts with little or no trouble. Unfortunately, oversized and inappropriately large colonies, due to their increased numbers, excrete increased amounts of these byproducts. As the amount of microbial byproducts increases, the higher waste byproducts levels can overburden the body's waste removal mechanisms.<ref name="DeGruttola_2016" />

A human's [[microbiome]] can change because of inflammatory processes, such as cell-mediated inflammation and host-mediated inflammation, or a 'driver' bacteria causing/aggravating inflammation. This change allows the microbial community to become more susceptible to pathogens. Once the pathogens are established successfully, they contribute to dysbiosis and produce [[Genotoxicity|genotoxins]] and other potential cancer-causing microbial metabolites.<ref name="Sheflin_2014">{{cite journal | vauthors = Sheflin AM, Whitney AK, Weir TL | title = Cancer-promoting effects of microbial dysbiosis | journal = Current Oncology Reports | volume = 16 | issue = 10 | article-number = 406 | date = October 2014 | pmid = 25123079 | pmc = 4180221 | doi = 10.1007/s11912-014-0406-0 }}</ref> The evolution of pathogens is another possible effect of dysbiosis, contributing to a potential increase in cancer risk.<ref name="Sheflin_2014" />

The density of bacteria in the [[Colon (anatomy)|colon]] is high (about 10<sup>12</sup> per ml.), and these bacteria are subject to dysbiosis.  By contrast, the [[small intestine]] has a relatively low density of bacteria (about 10<sup>2</sup> per ml.) so that dysbiosis is likely less of a problem.  This difference may account for the greater than 10-fold higher incidence of cancer in the colon compared to the small intestine.<ref name = Bernstein2022/> The risk of [[gastrointestinal cancer|intestinal cancer]] is associated with a Western high fat diet that increases susceptibility to secondary bile acid induced dysbiosis.<ref name = Cao2017>{{cite journal|pmid=28187526 |date=2017 |last1=Cao |first1=H. |last2=Xu |first2=M. |last3=Dong |first3=W. |last4=Deng |first4=B. |last5=Wang |first5=S. |last6=Zhang |first6=Y. |last7=Wang |first7=S. |last8=Luo |first8=S. |last9=Wang |first9=W. |last10=Qi |first10=Y. |last11=Gao |first11=J. |last12=Cao |first12=X. |last13=Yan |first13=F. |last14=Wang |first14=B. |title=Secondary bile acid-induced dysbiosis promotes intestinal carcinogenesis |journal=International Journal of Cancer |volume=140 |issue=11 |pages=2545–2556 |doi=10.1002/ijc.30643 |s2cid=19866323 |doi-access=free }}</ref>   Exposure of the colon to an increased level of secondary bile acids resulting from dysbiosis can cause [[DNA damage (naturally occurring)|DNA damage]], and such damage can cause carcinogenic mutations in colon cells.<ref name = Bernstein2022>{{cite journal|pmid=36408538 |date=2023 |last1=Bernstein |first1=H. |last2=Bernstein |first2=C. |title=Bile acids as carcinogens in the colon and at other sites in the gastrointestinal system |journal=Experimental Biology and Medicine |volume=248 |issue=1 |pages=79–89 |doi=10.1177/15353702221131858 |pmc=9989147 }}</ref>  

Gut dysbiosis can affect the cardiovascular system "via signaling molecules and bioactive metabolites.<ref name="Koszewicz_2021" /> This could cause diseases through neuro-entero-endocrine hormones that can lead to heart failure and other conditions such as chronic kidney disease, hypertension, obesity, and diabetes.<ref name="Koszewicz_2021" />

== Associated illnesses ==
Cross-regulation occurs between the host and the gut microbiota in healthy people, resulting in a homeostatic equilibrium of bacteria that keeps the gastrointestinal tract healthy and free of potentially pathogenic bacteria.<ref name="DeGruttola_2016">{{cite journal | vauthors = DeGruttola AK, Low D, Mizoguchi A, Mizoguchi E | title = Current Understanding of Dysbiosis in Disease in Human and Animal Models | journal = Inflammatory Bowel Diseases | volume = 22 | issue = 5 | pages = 1137–50 | date = May 2016 | pmid = 27070911 | pmc = 4838534 | doi = 10.1097/MIB.0000000000000750 }}</ref> There are three significant categories of dysbiosis: loss of beneficial organisms, excessive growth of potentially harmful microorganisms, and loss of overall microbial diversity.<ref name="DeGruttola_2016" /> Disruptions in the microbiome can allow outside factors or even pathogenic members of the microbiome to take hold in the gut environment. Dysbiosis has been reported to be associated with illnesses, such as multiple chemical sensitivity, periodontal disease,<ref name="Nath_2013">{{cite journal | vauthors = Nath SG, Raveendran R | title = Microbial dysbiosis in periodontitis | journal = Journal of Indian Society of Periodontology | volume = 17 | issue = 4 | pages = 543–45 | date = July 2013 | pmid = 24174742 | pmc = 3800425 | doi = 10.4103/0972-124X.118334 | doi-access = free }}</ref> [[inflammatory bowel disease]],<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Marteau P | title = Bacterial flora in inflammatory bowel disease | journal = Digestive Diseases | volume = 27 | issue = Suppl 1 | pages = 99–103 | year = 2009 | pmid = 20203504 | doi = 10.1159/000268128 | s2cid = 40843845 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Lepage P, Leclerc MC, Joossens M, Mondot S, Blottière HM, Raes J, Ehrlich D, Doré J | display-authors = 6 | title = A metagenomic insight into our gut's microbiome | journal = Gut | volume = 62 | issue = 1 | pages = 146–58 | date = January 2013 | pmid = 22525886 | doi = 10.1136/gutjnl-2011-301805 | s2cid = 206956549 }}</ref> [[chronic fatigue syndrome]],<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Lakhan SE, Kirchgessner A | title = Gut inflammation in chronic fatigue syndrome | journal = Nutrition & Metabolism | volume = 7 | page = 79 | date = October 2010 | pmid = 20939923 | pmc = 2964729 | doi = 10.1186/1743-7075-7-79 | doi-access = free }}</ref> [[obesity]],<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Turnbaugh PJ, Ley RE, Mahowald MA, Magrini V, Mardis ER, Gordon JI | title = An obesity-associated gut microbiome with increased capacity for energy harvest | journal = Nature | volume = 444 | issue = 7122 | pages = 1027–31 | date = December 2006 | pmid = 17183312 | doi = 10.1038/nature05414 | bibcode = 2006Natur.444.1027T | s2cid = 4400297 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Turnbaugh PJ, Hamady M, Yatsunenko T, Cantarel BL, Duncan A, Ley RE, Sogin ML, Jones WJ, Roe BA, Affourtit JP, Egholm M, Henrissat B, Heath AC, Knight R, Gordon JI | display-authors = 6 | title = A core gut microbiome in obese and lean twins | journal = Nature | volume = 457 | issue = 7228 | pages = 480–84 | date = January 2009 | pmid = 19043404 | pmc = 2677729 | doi = 10.1038/nature07540 | bibcode = 2009Natur.457..480T }}</ref> [[cancer]],<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Castellarin M, Warren RL, Freeman JD, Dreolini L, Krzywinski M, Strauss J, Barnes R, Watson P, Allen-Vercoe E, Moore RA, Holt RA | display-authors = 6 | title = Fusobacterium nucleatum infection is prevalent in human colorectal carcinoma | journal = Genome Research | volume = 22 | issue = 2 | pages = 299–306 | date = February 2012 | pmid = 22009989 | pmc = 3266037 | doi = 10.1101/gr.126516.111 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Kostic AD, Gevers D, Pedamallu CS, Michaud M, Duke F, Earl AM, Ojesina AI, Jung J, Bass AJ, Tabernero J, Baselga J, Liu C, Shivdasani RA, Ogino S, Birren BW, Huttenhower C, Garrett WS, Meyerson M | display-authors = 6 | title = Genomic analysis identifies association of Fusobacterium with colorectal carcinoma | journal = Genome Research | volume = 22 | issue = 2 | pages = 292–98 | date = February 2012 | pmid = 22009990 | pmc = 3266036 | doi = 10.1101/gr.126573.111 }}</ref> [[bacterial vaginosis]],<ref name="AfricaNel20142">{{cite journal | vauthors = Africa CW, Nel J, Stemmet M | title = Anaerobes and bacterial vaginosis in pregnancy: virulence factors contributing to vaginal colonisation | journal = International Journal of Environmental Research and Public Health | volume = 11 | issue = 7 | pages = 6979–7000 | date = July 2014 | pmid = 25014248 | pmc = 4113856 | doi = 10.3390/ijerph110706979 | doi-access = free }}</ref> and [[colitis]].<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Mazmanian SK | title = Capsular polysaccharides of symbiotic bacteria modulate immune responses during experimental colitis | journal = Journal of Pediatric Gastroenterology and Nutrition | volume = 46 | issue = Suppl 1 | pages = E11–12 | date = April 2008 | pmid = 18354314 | doi = 10.1097/01.mpg.0000313824.70971.a7 }}</ref>

=== Inflammatory bowel disease ===
There is no single or well-understood microbial cause of [[Crohn's disease]], but three major pathogens have been associated with Crohn's disease: ''[[Mycobacterium avium paratuberculosis]]'' (MAP), adherent-invasive ''[[Escherichia coli]]'' (AIEC), and ''[[Clostridioides difficile]]''.<ref name="DeGruttola_2016" /> The causal role, if any, these bacteria play has yet to be determined. Rather than the "one-microbe-one disease" hypothesis, some think that Crohn's is caused by an imbalance of commensal microflora associated with more complex interactions between the host and the entire intestinal microbiota.<ref name="DeGruttola_2016" />

=== Obesity ===
[[Obesity]] is a metabolic condition in which the body retains an unhealthy amount of fat.<ref name="DeGruttola_2016" /> Similar to IBD, a specific microbiota appears to be linked to the development of obesity. There is a notable reduction in microbial diversity in obese individuals. Research in humans and animals shows an association of obesity with altered ratios between [[Bacteroidetes]] and [[Firmicutes]]; as Bacteriodetes decreases, Firmicutes increases.<ref name="DeGruttola_2016" /> This ratio has been linked to body weight and fat accumulation, indicating that obese people have a higher disproportionate ratio of these bacteria.<ref name="DeGruttola_2016" />

=== Diabetes mellitus ===
[[Diabetes mellitus]] (DM) is a carbohydrate metabolism disorder characterized by insufficient insulin output or utilization, which is needed for the body to turn sugars and starches into energy. The prevalence of DM in the United States is about 29.1 million, with about 1.7 million new diagnoses annually.<ref name="DeGruttola_2016" /> The two forms of diabetes are Type 1 and Type 2. Type 1 DM is also known as insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus (IDDM). Type 1 diabetes is an autoimmune condition that affects the beta cells in the pancreas, causing insulin production to be impaired.<ref name="DeGruttola_2016" /> It is most often diagnosed in children and young adults. Type 2 diabetes mellitus, also known as non-insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus (NIDDM), is a type of diabetes that affects adults and is characterized by insulin resistance, which occurs when tissue sensitivity insulin is reduced, causing the body to ignore the insulin released. Research has shown dysbiosis of the intestinal microbiota may contribute to both forms of diabetes. Dysbiosis related to type 1 DM is characterized by a decline in mucin-degrading bacteria, such as ''Bifidobacteria'', ''Lactobacillus'', and ''Prevotella'', and an increase in ''Bacteroidetes'' and ''Clostridium''.<ref name="DeGruttola_2016" />

=== Cancer ===
Sustained periods of dysbiosis lead to extended amounts of stress and inflammation in the gut microbiome, which can in turn promote the production of carcinogenic metabolites.<ref name="Sheflin_2014"/> Intestinal dysbiosis has been associated with colorectal cancer (CRC). According to the American Cancer Society, colorectal cancer is the third most common cancer and the second leading cause of cancer death in the United States.<ref name="DeGruttola_2016" /> In CRC patients, a general dysbiosis pattern has been discovered, including a decrease in butyrate-producing bacteria and an increase in the proportion of several potentially pathogenic bacteria.<ref name="DeGruttola_2016" />

=== ''Clostridioides difficile'' ===
''[[C. difficile]]'' is an opportunistic bacteria that commonly infects patients following a disruption in the microbiome, such as treatment with antibiotics.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Knoop FC, Owens M, Crocker IC | title = Clostridium difficile: clinical disease and diagnosis | journal = Clinical Microbiology Reviews | volume = 6 | issue = 3 | pages = 251–65 | date = July 1993 | pmid = 8358706 | pmc = 358285 | doi = 10.1128/CMR.6.3.251 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Surawicz CM, Brandt LJ, Binion DG, Ananthakrishnan AN, Curry SR, Gilligan PH, McFarland LV, Mellow M, Zuckerbraun BS | display-authors = 6 | title = Guidelines for diagnosis, treatment, and prevention of Clostridium difficile infections | journal = The American Journal of Gastroenterology | volume = 108 | issue = 4 | pages = 478–98; quiz 499 | date = April 2013 | pmid = 23439232 | doi = 10.1038/ajg.2013.4 | s2cid = 54629762 | doi-access = free }}</ref> Infection can lead to several different symptoms including watery diarrhea, fever, loss of appetite, nausea, and abdominal pain.<ref>{{cite web | url = https://www.cdc.gov/hai/organisms/cdiff/cdiff-patient.html | title = Clostridium difficile Infection Information for Patients | publisher = Centers for Disease Control | access-date = 2018-06-27 | archive-date = 2017-03-30 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20170330023956/https://www.cdc.gov/hai/organisms/cdiff/Cdiff-patient.html | url-status = live }}</ref> Severe or chronic infections of ''C. difficile'' can lead to inflammation of the colon, a condition known as [[colitis]].<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Surawicz CM, McFarland LV, Elmer G, Chinn J | title = Treatment of recurrent Clostridium difficile colitis with vancomycin and Saccharomyces boulardii | journal = The American Journal of Gastroenterology | volume = 84 | issue = 10 | pages = 1285–87 | date = October 1989 | pmid = 2679049 }}</ref>

=== Periodontitis ===
[[Periodontitis]] is an oral infection that can damage the bones supporting teeth and lead to tooth loss.<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.mayoclinic.org/diseases-conditions/periodontitis/symptoms-causes/syc-20354473|title=Periodontitis – Symptoms and causes|work=Mayo Clinic|access-date=2018-06-27|archive-date=2019-06-06|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190606035235/https://www.mayoclinic.org/diseases-conditions/periodontitis/symptoms-causes/syc-20354473|url-status=live}}</ref> One of the major risk factors for periodontitis is the disruption of the oral microbiome such that there is an accumulation of pathogenic bacteria.<ref name="Nath_2013"/> Studies show that the oral microbiota changes as periodontitis progress, shifting from gram-positive aerobes to gram-negative anaerobes. Oral dysbiosis is likely to evolve, shifting the symbiotic host-microbe relationship to a pathogenic one. During this time, the host's oral health deteriorates, eventually leading to clinical disease.<ref name="Nath_2013" />

=== Acne vulgaris ===
The use of [[minocycline]] in acne vulgaris has been associated with skin and gut dysbiosis.<ref name="Thompson_2020" />

===Cardiovascular disease===

Dysbiosis of [[gut microbiota|intestinal microbiota]] may accelerate the progression of [[cardiovascular disease]].<ref name="Jin2019">{{Cite journal |pmid=30712327 |date=2019 |last1=Jin |first1=M. |last2=Qian |first2=Z. |last3=Yin |first3=J. |last4=Xu |first4=W. |last5=Zhou |first5=X. |title=The role of intestinal microbiota in cardiovascular disease |journal=Journal of Cellular and Molecular Medicine |volume=23 |issue=4 |pages=2343–2350 |doi=10.1111/jcmm.14195 |pmc=6433673 }}</ref> [[Diet (nutrition)|Dietary habits]], [[stress (biology)|high stress]], intestinal infections and use of antibiotics can cause an imbalance in the species and quantity of microorganisms in the adult intestine. Gut dysbiosis may lead to [[inflammation]] and metabolic disorders that promote the development of cardiovascular disease.<ref name = Jin2019/>

== Treatments ==
=== Antibiotics ===
{{See also|Antibiotic misuse}}
Because of the complex interactions in the microbiome, not much data exists on the efficacy of using antibiotics to treat dysbiosis. However, the broad-spectrum antibiotic [[rifaximin]] has been shown to have a favorable response in several of the ailments associated with dysbiosis, including [[irritable bowel syndrome]].<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Sharara AI, Aoun E, Abdul-Baki H, Mounzer R, Sidani S, Elhajj I | title = A randomized double-blind placebo-controlled trial of rifaximin in patients with abdominal bloating and flatulence | journal = The American Journal of Gastroenterology | volume = 101 | issue = 2 | pages = 326–33 | date = February 2006 | doi = 10.1111/j.1572-0241.2006.00458.x | pmid = 16454838 | s2cid = 23817453 }}</ref>

While most antibiotics alter the gut microbiota for the duration of the treatment, some cause long-lasting changes. However, repeated exposure to antibiotics can also cause the opposite of the intended effect and destabilize the gut microbiome, resulting in promoting "outgrowth of antibiotic-resistant pathogenic bacteria" (see [[antibiotic misuse]]) thus aggravating gut dysbiosis.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Weiss GA, Hennet T | title = Mechanisms and consequences of intestinal dysbiosis | journal = Cellular and Molecular Life Sciences | volume = 74 | issue = 16 | pages = 2959–2977 | date = August 2017 | pmid = 28352996 | doi = 10.1007/s00018-017-2509-x | pmc = 11107543 | s2cid = 22334485 | url = https://www.zora.uzh.ch/id/eprint/144519/1/144519.pdf | access-date = 2021-05-21 | archive-date = 2021-08-05 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20210805035856/https://www.zora.uzh.ch/id/eprint/144519/1/144519.pdf | url-status = live }}</ref>

=== Fecal microbiota transplant (FMT) ===
[[Fecal microbiota transplant|Fecal microbiota transplantation]] (FMT) is an experimental treatment that has resolved 80–90 percent of dysbiosis-related infections caused by recurrent ''C. difficile'' infections that do not respond to antibiotics in randomized, controlled clinical trials.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Gianotti RJ, Moss AC | title = The Use and Efficacy of Fecal Microbiota Transplantation for Refractory Clostridium difficile in Patients with Inflammatory Bowel Disease | journal = Inflammatory Bowel Diseases | volume = 22 | issue = 11 | pages = 2704–2710 | date = November 2016 | pmid = 27755271 | doi = 10.1097/mib.0000000000000950 | doi-access = free }}</ref> A patient's colon is transplanted during FMT with a fecal preparation from a carefully screened, healthy stool donor. FMT is thought to work by repopulating the patient's microbiome with various microorganisms that compete with ''C. difficile'' for space.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Giau VV, Lee H, An SS, Hulme J | title = Recent advances in the treatment of ''C. difficile'' using biotherapeutic agents | journal = Infection and Drug Resistance | volume = 12 | pages = 1597–1615 | date = June 2019 | pmid = 31354309 | pmc = 6579870 | doi = 10.2147/IDR.S207572 | doi-access = free }}</ref>

FMTs use the same line of reasoning as probiotics; to recreate a healthy balance of microbiota in the microbiome by inserting beneficial microbes into the environment. FMT accomplishes this by taking a donation of fecal matter from a healthy individual, diluted, strained and introduced to a diseased patient.<ref>{{cite web | url = http://thefecaltransplantfoundation.org/what-is-fecal-transplant/ | title = What is FMT? – The Fecal Transplant Foundation | website = thefecaltransplantfoundation.org | access-date = 2018-06-27 | archive-date = 2019-09-01 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20190901233018/http://thefecaltransplantfoundation.org/what-is-fecal-transplant/ | url-status = live }}</ref> FMTs are currently used to treat patients with ''Clostridioides difficile'' infections, who have proved resistant to other therapies.;<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Smith MB, Kelly C, Alm EJ | title = Policy: How to regulate faecal transplants | journal = Nature | volume = 506 | issue = 7488 | pages = 290–291 | date = February 2014 | pmid = 24558658 | doi = 10.1038/506290a | doi-access = free }}</ref> however, this is considered an investigational therapy at present with risks that have not been fully defined.<ref>{{Cite web | url=https://www.idsociety.org/public-health/emerging-clinical-issues/emerging-clinical-issues/fecal-microbiota-transplantation/ | title=Fecal Microbiota Transplantation | work=Emerging Infections Network | date=2022 | access-date=2020-04-13 | archive-date=2019-09-13 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190913205820/https://www.idsociety.org/public-health/emerging-clinical-issues/emerging-clinical-issues/fecal-microbiota-transplantation/ | url-status=live }}</ref> FMT is also being investigated for use in psychiatric disorders.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Chinna Meyyappan A, Forth E, Wallace CJ, Milev R | title = Effect of fecal microbiota transplant on symptoms of psychiatric disorders: a systematic review | journal = BMC Psychiatry | volume = 20 | issue = 1 | article-number = 299 | date = June 2020 | pmid = 32539741 | pmc = 7294648 | doi = 10.1186/s12888-020-02654-5 | doi-access = free }}</ref> Because the process is not sterile and contaminations can pass from donor to patient, there is a push to isolate key microbiota and culture them independently.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Dupont HL | title = Diagnosis and management of Clostridium difficile infection | language = English | journal = Clinical Gastroenterology and Hepatology | volume = 11 | issue = 10 | pages = 1216–23; quiz e73 | date = October 2013 | pmid = 23542332 | doi = 10.1016/j.cgh.2013.03.016 | doi-access = free }}</ref>

=== Probiotics ===
The World Health Organization defines [[probiotic]]s as "live microorganisms, which when administered in adequate amounts, confer a health benefit on the host".<ref>Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations; World Health Organization. Health and Nutritional Properties of Probiotics in Food Including Powder Milk with Live Lactic Acid Bacteria.Córdoba, Argentina: Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations, World Health Organization; 2001.</ref> The benefit of using probiotics to treat dysbiosis related diseases lies in its ability to treat the underlying cause of said diseases. Some benefits include their ability to suppress inflammation in the microbiome<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Drakes M, Blanchard T, Czinn S | title = Bacterial probiotic modulation of dendritic cells | journal = Infection and Immunity | volume = 72 | issue = 6 | pages = 3299–309 | date = June 2004 | pmid = 15155633 | pmc = 415669 | doi = 10.1128/IAI.72.6.3299-3309.2004 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Kim SO, Sheikh HI, Ha SD, Martins A, Reid G | title = G-CSF-mediated inhibition of JNK is a key mechanism for Lactobacillus rhamnosus-induced suppression of TNF production in macrophages | journal = Cellular Microbiology | volume = 8 | issue = 12 | pages = 1958–71 | date = December 2006 | pmid = 16889627 | doi = 10.1111/j.1462-5822.2006.00763.x | doi-access = free }}</ref> and disrupt colonization by pathogens.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Kendall MM, Sperandio V | title = Quorum sensing by enteric pathogens | journal = Current Opinion in Gastroenterology | volume = 23 | issue = 1 | pages = 10–15 | date = January 2007 | pmid = 17133078 | doi = 10.1097/MOG.0b013e3280118289 | s2cid = 45681413 }}</ref>

Excessive use of antibiotics, IBD, obesity, diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and many more ailments are related to interruptions in the microbiome(dysbiosis), especially in the human gut. Probiotics can promote healthier microbial function by introducing or reintroducing helpful bacteria to strengthen the weaknesses presented in a dysbiotic microbiome.<ref name="Wischmeyer_2016">{{cite journal | vauthors = Wischmeyer PE, McDonald D, Knight R | title = Role of the microbiome, probiotics, and 'dysbiosis therapy' in critical illness | language = en-US | journal = Current Opinion in Critical Care | volume = 22 | issue = 4 | pages = 347–53 | date = August 2016 | pmid = 27327243 | pmc = 5065053 | doi = 10.1097/MCC.0000000000000321 }}</ref> It is essential to recognize that such circumstances are beneficial bacteria that occur more frequently than harmful ones. Probiotics can be utilized in aiding existing conditions and preventing such diseases by instituting anti-inflammatory properties and improving immune cell function.<ref name="Wischmeyer_2016" />

The human gut contains a wide diversity of bacteria and can easily be disrupted through diet, medicinal usage, diseases, and many others. Probiotics have proven influential in returning the intestinal microbiota to homeostatic balance and improve intestinal health.<ref name="Kim_2019">{{cite journal | vauthors = Kim SK, Guevarra RB, Kim YT, Kwon J, Kim H, Cho JH, Kim HB, Lee JH | display-authors = 6 | title = Role of Probiotics in Human Gut Microbiome-Associated Diseases | journal = Journal of Microbiology and Biotechnology | volume = 29 | issue = 9 | pages = 1335–1340 | date = September 2019 | pmid = 31434172 | doi = 10.4014/jmb.1906.06064 | doi-access = free }}</ref>

Probiotics contain anti-inflammatory properties that assist in the prevention and treatment of intestinal diseases due to microbial dysbiosis. More research is needed to understand better the many benefits probiotics can offer for multiple forms of dysbiosis.<ref name="Kim_2019"/> ''[[Lactobacillus]]'' is the most researched genus of probiotic bacteria.<ref name="pmid35464397">{{cite journal | vauthors = Dempsey E, Corr SC | title = Lactobacillus spp. for Gastrointestinal Health: Current and Future Perspectives | journal = Frontiers in Immunology | volume = 13 | issue = | article-number = 840245 | date = 2022 | pmid = 35464397 | pmc = 9019120 | doi = 10.3389/fimmu.2022.840245 | url = | doi-access = free }}</ref> It is sold to consumers for gut health either as a single strain or part of a multi-strain formulation.

== See also ==

* [[List of bacterial vaginosis microbiota]]
